text: The surgeon mobilizes the descending and transverse colon, focusing on the splenic flexure for an ultra-low rectal cancer anastomosis. The inferior mesenteric artery is identified by lifting the sigmoid colon and dividing the peritoneum at the sacral promontory, while safeguarding the left ureter and autonomic nerves. The gonadal vessels are pushed down, and the descending colon is lifted to the lateral abdominal wall. The inferior mesenteric vein is divided, and the mesocolon is dissected along the pancreatic body and tail, reaching the spleen. The splenic flexure is lifted, and the transverse mesocolon is divided medially to open the lesser sac, revealing the stomach. The distal transverse colon is freed from the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon returns to the pelvic region, identifying the left autonomic nerve while lifting the rectum. The right autonomic nerve is visible, and dissection proceeds anterior and medial to these nerves, safeguarding the ureters. Using either monopolar or harmonic energy sources, the surgeon continues dissection into the pre-circular hollow, focusing on the loose tissue area on the right side, medial to the right autonomic nerve bundle.

The surgeon uses a vascular ligating instrument, such as a harmonic or ligature, to manage small vessels encountered while dissecting lower into the pelvic region. The complete mesocolic and mesorectal tissues are removed, maintaining the integrity of the mesorectal plane. The dissection continues until reaching the pelvic floor, where the muscles are visible and clean of surrounding tissues.

The surgeon proceeds with dissection on the left side, having completed most posterior dissection from the right. The left ureter is identified, and the peritoneum is divided medial to the ovarian pedicle, which is pushed laterally to safeguard the autonomic nerve bundles. The lateral support of the rectum is divided to encompass lymphatic tissues, moving lower and more laterally below the main nerve bundles. In this female patient, the uterus is lifted using a uterine manipulator or sutured to the anterior abdominal wall. Tissue is divided between the posterior vaginal wall and the anterior rectal wall.

The surgeon identifies the level of rectal dissection and thins out the rectal tube by dividing lateral fatty tissues. This minimizes the use of staples, reducing the risk of ischemia and leakage. The rectum is divided at a low level, confirmed to be beyond the small tumor by palpation, using a single firing of a green or gold cartridge.

In a narrow male android pelvis, the surgeon uses multiple firings to divide the low rectum. The left lateral peritoneal attachment of the descending colon is taken down using a harmonic device. The gastrocolic ligament is divided, specifically the distal half, ensuring the mesocolon is freed from the retroperitoneal area. A small suprapubic incision is made, and the specimen is brought out, inserted into the colon, and anastomosis is performed. The remaining colon is easily maneuvered into the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon inspects the anastomotic line to confirm the completeness of the donor sites. A leak test is performed to ensure there is no leakage, and the absence of leaks negates the need for a colostomy.
